generations.code:
This is equivalent to: the union of all S_i = {1,...,n} (all friends can be served), AND there exists a way to cover all friends with ≤ n-1 shops.

If any shop covers ≥ 2 friends, we can potentially use it to save one shop slot. 

Actually: if we can cover all friends with ≤ n-1 shops, it's feasible. The greedy approach: we need to cover n friends with n-1 shops, so at least one shop must cover ≥ 2 friends.

The minimum number of shops needed to cover all friends = set cover minimum. But set cover is NP-hard in general... however n can be large.

Wait, but we just need ≤ n-1 shops. The only way this FAILS is if every friend can only be served by a unique shop (each friend requires a distinct shop), meaning we need exactly n shops. That happens when for each friend j, only one shop can serve them with joy ≥ α, and all these shops are distinct.

So the check fails only if: the friends that can only be served by exactly one shop, and those shops are all distinct AND together they require n distinct shops total... Actually it fails if minimum set cover size > n-1, i.e., ≥ n.

Simpler: it's feasible if there exists at least one shop that covers ≥ 2 friends (among the friends that are "covered" by at least one shop), AND all friends are covered.

Wait no. Consider n=3: friends A,B,C. A→shop1 only, B→shop2 only, C→shop3 only. Minimum shops = 3 = n, so n-1=2 is not enough.

If A→shop1 only, B→shop1 or shop2, C→shop3 only. Min shops: shop1 covers A,B; shop3 covers C. Total = 2 = n-1. Feasible.

So the check: all friends covered AND minimum set cover ≤ n-1.

For minimum set cover ≤ n-1: this equals "NOT (minimum set cover = n)", which means NOT "every friend is covered by a unique shop with no sharing possible."

Actually min set cover ≤ n-1 iff there exists a shop covering ≥ 2 friends (when we greedily pick). Hmm, not exactly.

Min set cover ≤ n-1 iff NOT all friends require completely disjoint shops. More precisely, min cover < n iff the sets are not all singletons forming a partition... 

Let me think differently: min cover ≤ n-1 iff there exist two friends j1, j2 that share at least one common shop (both have p_{i,j1} ≥ α and p_{i,j2} ≥ α for some i).

Because if any two friends share a shop, we can cover them together, saving one shop, giving ≤ n-1 total.

So: feasible iff (all friends covered) AND (some shop covers ≥ 2 friends).

Binary search on α with these checks!

If any shop covers ≥ 2 friends, we can potentially use it to save one shop slot. 

Actually: if we can cover all friends with ≤ n-1 shops, it's feasible. The greedy approach: we need to cover n friends with n-1 shops, so at least one shop must cover ≥ 2 friends.

The minimum number of shops needed to cover all friends = set cover minimum. But set cover is NP-hard in general... however n can be large.

Wait, but we just need ≤ n-1 shops. The only way this FAILS is if every friend can only be served by a unique shop (each friend requires a distinct shop), meaning we need exactly n shops. That happens when for each friend j, only one shop can serve them with joy ≥ α, and all these shops are distinct.

So the check fails only if: the friends that can only be served by exactly one shop, and those shops are all distinct AND together they require n distinct shops total... Actually it fails if minimum set cover size > n-1, i.e., ≥ n.

Simpler: it's feasible if there exists at least one shop that covers ≥ 2 friends (among the friends that are "covered" by at least one shop), AND all friends are covered.

Wait no. Consider n=3: friends A,B,C. A→shop1 only, B→shop2 only, C→shop3 only. Minimum shops = 3 = n, so n-1=2 is not enough.

If A→shop1 only, B→shop1 or shop2, C→shop3 only. Min shops: shop1 covers A,B; shop3 covers C. Total = 2 = n-1. Feasible.

So the check: all friends covered AND minimum set cover ≤ n-1.

For minimum set cover ≤ n-1: this equals "NOT (minimum set cover = n)", which means NOT "every friend is covered by a unique shop with no sharing possible."

Actually min set cover ≤ n-1 iff there exists a shop covering ≥ 2 friends (when we greedily pick). Hmm, not exactly.

Min set cover ≤ n-1 iff NOT all friends require completely disjoint shops. More precisely, min cover < n iff the sets are not all singletons forming a partition... 

Let me think differently: min cover ≤ n-1 iff there exist two friends j1, j2 that share at least one common shop (both have p_{i,j1} ≥ α and p_{i,j2} ≥ α for some i).

Because if any two friends share a shop, we can cover them together, saving one shop, giving ≤ n-1 total.

So: feasible iff (all friends covered) AND (some shop covers ≥ 2 friends).
